column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Serpentine Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/stone/sedimentary/serpentine-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Serpentine cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Serpentine mineral absorption
Beam spot diameter for precise Serpentine surface cleaning
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage
Minimum fluence required for effective Serpentine ablation
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient ablation of Serpentine layers
Optimal scanning speed for efficient Serpentine removal
Recommended number of passes for complete Serpentine removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform Serpentine cleaning

Typical range for Nd:YAG laser cleaning of silicates like serpentine to remove contaminants without substrate ablation; starting at 100W for light contamination. Max avoids thermal damage given serpentine's low thermal conductivity (~2 W/m·K). Source: General manufacturer specs (e.g., IPG Photonics systems for stone cleaning) and studies on mineral ablation (e.g., Paljk et al., J. Cult. Herit., 2010).

Effective for contaminant removal on serpentine; slower for heavy contamination (200-500 mm/s), faster for light (1000-2000 mm/s). Recommended balances cleaning efficiency and heat input. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt-Laser systems) and process optimization for stone surfaces.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of serpentine with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ further but less common industrially. Avoids excessive melting (serpentine melting ~1100°C). Source: Laser physics research on silicate ablation (e.g., Anisimov et al., Appl. Surf. Sci., 1999).

Typical for pulsed cleaning to control heat accumulation on low-conductivity serpentine; higher rates speed up process but risk thermal buildup. Recommended for balanced throughput. Source: Academic literature on pulse train effects in stone cleaning (e.g., Pouli et al., Lasers Archaeol., 2007).

Fluence range for contaminant ablation without exceeding serpentine damage threshold (~3 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate reflectivity ~0.3-0.5). Recommended for safe surface preparation. Conservative estimate for uncertain exact threshold. Source: Ablation threshold studies on minerals (e.g., Lazic et al., Spectrochim. Acta B, 2003).

Beam diameter for uniform cleaning; smaller for fine details on serpentine surfaces, larger for broad areas. Recommended for industrial contamination removal without pinpoint damage. Source: Equipment specifications from laser cleaning heads (e.g., Laserax or Coherent systems).

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; recommended for complete removal on serpentine while minimizing exposure time and potential microcracking. Source: Industry case studies on stone restoration (e.g., European Commission reports on laser cleaning of cultural heritage stones, 2015).

Ensures uniform coverage without excessive overlap-induced heating on serpentine; 50-70% optimal for even contaminant removal. Source: Process optimization studies in laser scanning (e.g., Stournaras et al., J. Manuf. Process., 2015).
